text: The biological rationale for linking increased oxidative stress with an increase in mitochondrial dysfunction is strong. This is supported by a variety of articles (Bhatti, Bhatti, and Reddy, 2017).   ROS molecules attack proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids non-specifically, which then continues to cause increased mitochondrial dysfunction (Kruidering et al., 1997). One example of a sign of mitochondrial dysfunction is the opening of the MPT pore, which is induced by calcium overload within the mitochondria, elevated phosphate levels, adenine nucleotide depletion, and conditions of oxidative stress (Belyaeva et al., 2012). Calcium homoeostasis proteins are known to be particularly vulnerable to ROS attack, resulting in oxidative stress leading to calcium excess in the mitochondria (Guo et al., 2013). Increased oxidative stress is also known to lead to changes in membrane potential and changes in antioxidant gene expression for genes such as superoxide dismutase (SOD) (Huerta-García et al., 2014). Mitochondrial changes occur in situations of increased oxidative stress due to the increased content of oxidized macromolecules within the cell as oxidative stress progresses (Guo et al., 2013).
